coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots-oriented synthesis of fungus-like CoP microspheres as a bifunctional electrocatalyst for efficient overall water splitting
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Hui
Liu, Zihui
Wang, Yang
Zhang, Jinqiang
Yang, Zhongxue
Hu, Han
Zhao, Qingshan
Ning, Hui
Zhi, Linjie
Wu, Mingbo
coredata.dc:description: The development and optimization of transition metal-based bifunctional electrocatalysts for both hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) and oxygen evolution reaction (OER) are critical for high-efficient water splitting. Herein, we propose a carbon dots-oriented strategy for fabricating nitrogen-doped carbon dots/fungus-like CoP microsphere composite on Ni foam (CoP-NCDs/NF). Owing to its unique structure and the strong interactions between carbon dots and CoP, the newly developed CoP-NCDs/NF electrode exhibits significantly enhanced HER and OER bifunctional catalytic activities, with overpotentials as low as 103 and 226 mV at a current density of 10 mA cm−2, respectively. Accordingly, the CoP-NCDs/NF displays excellent overall water splitting performance with a low cell voltage of 1.55 V at 10 mA cm−2 and great stability in a two-electrode alkaline electrolyzer. This study provides an alternative pathway on rationally designing and fabricating carbon dots-based materials for high performance water electrolysis and other energy conversion fields.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Transition metal phosphide
Hydrogen evolution reaction
Oxygen evolution reaction
Overall water splitting

Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We acknowledge support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 52072409 , U1662113 , U20A20131 ), Shandong Provincial Natural Science Foundation ( ZR2018ZC1458 ), and Taishan Scholar Project (No. ts201712020 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A–C) SEM images, (D–E) TEM images, (F) HRTEM image and (G) EDX mapping of CoP-NCDs-0.5/NF (Fig. 1C inset: model image of fungus-like CoP-NCDs-0.5/NF). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
SEM and TEM images of (A, B, C) CoP/NF, (D, E, F) CoP-NCDs-0.2/NF, and (G, H, I) CoP-NCDs-1.0/NF. (inset: model images of CoP/NF, CoP-NCDs-0.2/NF, and CoP-NCDs-1.0/NF, respectively).

(A) HER polarization curves and (B) corresponding Tafel plots of CoP-NCDs-0.5/NF, CoP/NF, bare Ni foam and Pt/C in 1.0 M KOH. (C) Durability test for CoP-NCDs-0.5/NF in 1.0 M KOH before and after 1000 cycles; inset: long-term electrochemical durability of CoP-NCDs-0.5/NF for the HER for 24 h. (D) OER polarization curves and (E) corresponding Tafel plots of CoP-NCDs-0.5/NF, CoP/NF, bare Ni foam and Pt/C in 1.0 M KOH. (F) Durability test for CoP-NCDs-0.5/NF in 1.0 M KOH before and after 1000 cycles; inset: long-term electrochemical durability of CoP-NCDs-0.5/NF for the OER for 24 h. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) XPS survey spectra, (B) Co 2p core level spectra, and (C) P 2p core level spectra of CoP-NCDs-0.5/NF and CoP/NF and (D) N 1s core level spectrum of CoP-NCDs-0.5/NF. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) Polarization curves of CoP-NCDs-0.5/NF||CoP-NCDs-0.5/NF, CoP/NF||CoP/NF, RuO2||Pt/C, and NF||NF for overall water splitting obtained in a two-electrode cell in 1.0 M KOH. (B) Long-term durability test of overall water splitting under a static overpotential of 1.55 and 1.60 V for CoP-NCDs-0.5/NF||CoP-NCDs-0.5/NF. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Schematic illustration for the formation of CoP-NCDs/NF catalyst.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots-oriented synthesis of fungus-like CoP microspheres as a bifunctional electrocatalyst for efficient overall water splitting

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing, College of New Energy, College of Chemical Engineering, China University of Petroleum (East China), Qingdao, 266580, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing, College of New Energy, College of Chemical Engineering, China University of Petroleum (East China), Qingdao, 266580, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Engineering, Edith Cowan University, 270 Joondalup Drive, Joondalup, WA, 6027, Australia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Engineering, Edith Cowan University, 270 Joondalup Drive, Joondalup, WA 6027, Australia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: CAS Key Laboratory of Nanosystem and Hierarchical Fabrication, CAS Center for Excellence in Nanoscience, National Center for Nanoscience and Technology, Beijing, 100190, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CAS Key Laboratory of Nanosystem and Hierarchical Fabrication
CAS Center for Excellence in Nanoscience
National Center for Nanoscience and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: CAS Key Laboratory of Nanosystem and Hierarchical Fabrication, CAS Center for Excellence in Nanoscience, National Center for Nanoscience and Technology, Beijing, 100190, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development and optimization of transition metal-based bifunctional electrocatalysts for both hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) and oxygen evolution reaction (OER) are critical for high-efficient water splitting. Herein, we propose a carbon dots-oriented strategy for fabricating nitrogen-doped carbon dots/fungus-like CoP microsphere composite on Ni foam (CoP-NCDs/NF). Owing to its unique structure and the strong interactions between carbon dots and CoP, the newly developed CoP-NCDs/NF electrode exhibits significantly enhanced HER and OER bifunctional catalytic activities, with overpotentials as low as 103 and 226 mV at a current density of 10 mA cm−2, respectively. Accordingly, the CoP-NCDs/NF displays excellent overall water splitting performance with a low cell voltage of 1.55 V at 10 mA cm−2 and great stability in a two-electrode alkaline electrolyzer. This study provides an alternative pathway on rationally designing and fabricating carbon dots-based materials for high performance water electrolysis and other energy conversion fields.

ce:section.ce:para: It is believed that hydrogen as one kind of renewable and sustainable energy will have a revolutionizing impact on the future energy-related applications [1,2]. Electrochemical water splitting, including hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) and oxygen evolution reaction (OER), is generally considered as an efficient pathway to generate high quality hydrogen energy without impurities. Owing to the unfavorable thermodynamics and intrinsic sluggish kinetics of these reactions, high-efficient electrocatalysts are urgently needed to stimulate and accelerate the overall water splitting process [3,4]. Although platinum and ruthenium based catalysts have exhibited favorable activities on HER and OER, respectively, the commercial applications have been largely restricted with their scarcity and high price. Accordingly, it is particularly attractive to develop low-cost and high-activity electrocatalysts for both HER and OER in alkaline electrolyte [5].

Recently, transition metal phosphides (TMP) have been considered as bifunctional HER and OER electrocatalysts under alkaline conditions as a result of their intriguing structural and electronic characters [6]. However, the finite active sites, the low intrinsic activity, and the poor stability greatly hamper their practical applications. Although numerous optimization strategies, such as introducing carbon components [7–9], composition regulation [10–14], and interface engineering [15–18] have been reported, the TMP based catalytic performances for overall water splitting are still unsatisfactory.

Carbon dots (CDs), as zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials, have gained great interests due to their characteristic properties, i.e., abundant edge defects, high surface area, enhanced charge-transfer efficiency, tunable optical properties and tightly integration capability with other nanomaterials [19–25]. The functional groups on the surfaces of CDs, such as –OH and –COOH, can provide sufficient active sites, and hence tremendously increase the catalytic performances of the electrocatalyst. Recent studies have revealed the promising potential of CDs as auxiliary materials to modulate the electronic characteristics of CDs-based composites for electrocatalysis [26–34]. Significantly, another merit of CDs is their natural property of small size, which enables their feasible integration with hybrid into various morphologies. This strategy has been successfully applied into energy storage [35,36], but rarely reported in the field of electrocatalysis.

Herein, we demonstrate a strategy for the fabrication of a novel hierarchical structure made of 3D porous conductive Ni foam covered with fungus-like CoP microspheres (CoP-NCDs/NF) by adjusting the dosage of structure-directing agent N-doped CDs. Interestingly, such a fungus-like CoP-NCDs/NF based electrode holds superior bifunctional electrocatalytic activities, achieving a current density of 10 mA cm−2 at very low overpotential of 103 mV (η10) for HER and low η10 value of 226 mV for OER in alkaline media. Moreover, this catalyst can afford a stable two-electrode for alkaline overall water splitting with a current density of 10 mA cm−2 at even a low cell voltage of 1.55 V. These outstanding electrocatalytic performances owe much to the specific electrode structure with high surface area and the increased electrical conductivity via the introduction of NCDs.

Ni foam (NF, 1.5 mm thickness) was purchased from Suzhou Jia Shi De Foam Metal Co., Ltd. Co(NO3)2·6H2O (AR, 98.5%), CO(NH2)2 (AR, 99.0%), NaH2PO2 (AR, 99.0%), Pt/C (20 wt% Pt on Vulcan XC-72R), RuO2, Nafion (5 wt%) and KOH (HPLC, 45 wt%) were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd. Milli-Q ultrapure water was used in all the experiments.

NCDs were synthesized from petroleum coke following an electrochemical method developed in our group [37]. Briefly, petroleum coke was first made into electrode disks with 2.8 cm in diameter and 2 mm in thickness by rolling and calcination. The prepared petroleum coke electrodes were employed as both negative and positive electrodes into 150 mL ammonia solution (5 wt%). Static potential of 15 V was applied to the two electrodes. After 4 h electrochemical reaction, the electrolyte solution was filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane to remove the black precipitate, and heated in a 40 °C water bath to evaporate ammonia. Finally, the NCDs powder was obtained by freeze-drying.

The fungus-like CoP-NCDs/NF samples were synthesized using a two-step method of hydrothermal and calcination processes. Briefly, a 0.5 mg mL−1 NCDs solution was first prepared with 25 mg NCDs powder dispersed into 50 mL deionized water, followed by adding 0.655 g Co(NO3)2 6H2O and 0.675 g urea under continuous stirring. After full dissolution, the solution and a piece of pretreated Ni foam (1 cm × 2 cm) were transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave for hydrothermal reaction. After being treated at 120 °C for 12 h, the Ni foam with Co precursor was taken out from the reactor, followed by being washed thoroughly with water and ethanol and dried at room temperature. Then the Ni foam with Co precursor (downstream) and 2.5 g NaH2PO2 (upstream) were separately placed in the tube furnace, which were subsequently heated for 150 min at 300 °C under N2 flow and then cooled down to room temperature. After cleaned by water and ethanol, the fungus-like CoP-NCDs-0.5/NF sample was obtained. In contrast, other samples were prepared using the similar methods with CoP-NCDs-0.5/NF except adding different concentration of NCDs (0, 0.2 and 1.0 mg mL−1) during the hydrothermal process. The obtained samples were denoted as CoP/NF, CoP-NCDs-0.2/NF, and CoP-NCDs-1.0/NF, respectively. In addition, Pt/C and RuO2 were coated on Ni foam with a loading of 3.6 mg cm−2 as benchmark, respectively.

All electrochemical experiments were performed with a CHI 760E electrochemical workstation (CH Instruments, Shanghai, China). For a typical three-electrode system, the freshly-prepared materials (1 cm × 1 cm) were served as the working electrode with the saturated calomel electrode (Ag/AgCl) and the graphite rod as reference electrode and counter electrode, respectively. Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) measurements were carried out in 1.0 M N2-saturated KOH for HER and 1.0 M O2-saturated KOH for OER at a same scan rate of 2 mV s−1. EIS results were obtained within the frequency range of 0.01–100000 Hz at corresponding conditions. The double-layer capacitance behavior (Cdl) was measured through the cyclic voltammograms curves (CVs), which were recorded at various scan rates (20, 40, 60, 80 and 100 mV s−1). The long-time stability was investigated by chronoamperometry at a constant applied overpotential. All potentials tested were converted to the reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE, ERHE = EAg/AgCl + 0.197 V + 0.059 pH). The data were all recorded without iR-compensation. A two-electrode system was constructed with CoP-NCD/NF electrode as both anode and cathode to evaluate the overall water splitting performance of the catalyst.

The microstructures of the catalysts were recognized by scanning electron microscope (SEM) and transmission electron microscope (TEM) coupled with an energy dispersive X-ray detector (EDX) spectrometer. The crystal structures of catalysts were characterized by a PANalytical X'Pert Pro MPD X-ray diffractometer (XRD). Raman spectra were recorded on a Renishaw RM2000 micro-Raman spectrometer. The infrared spectra were collected by a Thermo Nicolet Corporation Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR). The Brunner-Emmet-Teller (BET) specific surface areas were investigated by the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms using a Micromeritics ASAP 2020 analyzer. The elemental composition and electronic energy level structure of catalysts were identified by a Thermo scientific ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer. The binding energy was calibrated by the C 1s peak at 284.8 eV. PL spectra were measured on a spec-trofluorometer.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images and the estimated size distribution (Fig. S1A and its top right inset) show fairly uniform NCDs with a size distribution of 4.36 ± 0.15 nm. High-resolution TEM image (Fig. S1A bottom right inset) elucidates a clear lattice spacing of 0.340 nm for the (002) plane of graphite [38], which can be further proved by the 26.5° peak on X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern (Fig. S1B). The X-ray photoelectron spectrum (XPS) of the NCDs indicates the existence of C, N, and O elements with contents of 58.02, 7.83, and 34.15 at%, respectively (Fig. S1C). Five peaks at 284.1 eV (CC/C–C), 285.9 eV (C–N), 284.7 eV (C–O), 287.8 eV (CO/CN) and 288.7 eV (O–CO) [33,39] can be distinguished in the high-resolution C 1s XPS spectrum (Fig. S1D), corresponding well with the Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectrum results (Fig. S1F). Three peaks for pyridinic N (398.4 eV), pyrrolic N (399.6 eV), and graphitic N (401.5 eV) can be fitted in the high-resolution N 1s XPS spectrum (Fig. S1E) [40]. The presence of O- and N-rich functional groups confers CDs with diverse ligand systems and sufficient metal binding sites, thus producing a local impact on the electronic structure of the hybrid.

The synthetic route of the fungus-like CoP-NCDs-0.5/NF is illustrated in Scheme 1 . NCDs (0.5 mg mL−1) was first added in the hydrothermal process of Co-carbonates hydroxide hydrate on Ni foam (denoted as CoCOH-NCDs/NF). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern in Fig. S2A displays that the diffraction peaks of CoCOH-NCDs/NF are similar to that of Co(CO3)0·5(OH)·0.11H2O (JCPDS: 48–0083) apart from the two strong characteristic peaks at 44.2° and 52° of the Ni foam (JCPDS: 70–0989) [41]. The formation of the Co-carbonates hydroxide hydrate is the prerequisite for the oriented synthesis of metal phosphides with NaH2PO2 as the phosphorus source. The complete transformation of CoCOH-NCDs/NF into CoP-NCDs/NF can be evidenced by the XRD diffraction peaks at 31.6°, 35.3°, 36.3°, 46.2°, 48.1°, 56.0° and 56.7°, which are assigned to (011), (200), (111), (112), (211), (020) and (301) phases of CoP (JCPDS: 29–0497, Fig. S3). Moreover, the weak and broad peak at 26.5° can be corresponded to the (002) lattice facet of the NCDs. The low-magnification SEM image (Fig. 1 A) shows that the interconnected fungus-like CoP-NCDs microspheres densely covered the entire surface of Ni foam. It can be observed that the obtained CoP-NCDs-0.5/NF maintains the fungus-like hierarchical micro/nanostructure of CoCOH-NCDs/NF (Fig. S2B-C), suggesting the excellent structural stability of the precursor. High-magnification SEM image (Fig. 1B) displays the individual microsphere of the fungus-like CoP-NCDs with a uniform diameter of 6–7 μm. Importantly, there are numerous side branches radially protruded from the center (Fig. 1C and inset), which could provide sufficient active areas and offer favorable contact between catalytic sites and electrolyte. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images further confirm the fungus-like morphologies of the CoP-NCDs products with plentiful of side branches at diameters of 25–40 nm (Fig. 1D–E). Moreover, Fig. 1F demonstrates the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of the branch, in which the characteristic spacings of 0.283 nm and 0.340 nm match with the (011) plane of CoP crystal and (002) plane of NCDs, respectively. Elemental mappings (Fig. 1G) also clearly show the distribution of Co, P, C, N, and O elements in the CoP-NCDs sample.

To study the forming mechanism of this special structure of CoP-NCDs, different concentrations of NCDs were added during the hydrothermal process and the products with different morphologies were monitored by SEM and TEM techniques. Fig. 2 A–C reveal that the as-obtained CoP/NF exhibits urchin-like microspheres with linear nanoneedles in the absence of NCDs, which is similar to that reported in the literature [42]. Surprisingly, when the concentration of NCDs is increased to 0.2 mg mL−1, the urchin microspheres grow bigger, accompanied by a few side branches around linear nanoneedles (named as CoP-NCDs-0.2/NF, Fig. 2D–F). When more NCDs (0.5 mg mL−1) are added during the synthesis process, these side branches become intensive and the obtained CoP-NCDs looks like villous fungus (named as CoP-NCDs-0.5/NF, Fig. 1A–D), whereas too much NCDs (1.0 mg mL−1) lead to the crisscross of side branches, resulting in the spherical structures with rough surface (named as CoP-NCDs-1.0/NF, Fig. 2G–I). These phenomena indicate that carbon dots play an important role in the morphology control owing to the plentiful of hydroxyl and carbonyl functional groups of NCDs [35].

Based on these results, a feasible CDs-oriented mechanism for CoP-NCDs/NF products with different morphologies is proposed. Urea can be hydrolyzed into hydroxyl and carbonate ions during the hydrothermal reaction, which thus was employed as anionic coordinating agent in this work [43]. Without NCDs, Co precursor crystal nucleus grew and its surface was reconstructed, leading to the formation of urchin-like CoP with regular nanoneedles after the phosphatization process. When NCDs were introduced into the system, they would combine with Co2+ to provide more formation sites for crystal nucleus due to the selective adsorption effects from the carbonyl and hydroxyl functional groups of NCDs. The anisotropic growth of newly formed crystal nucleus was then oriented, which finally grew into fungus-like structure with extended branches around the nanoneedles. Nevertheless, the excessive addition of NCDs seriously disrupted the crystal growth along a certain direction, forcing the formation of spherical structure to decrease the surface energy [44]. Similar peaks correspond to CoP crystals were observed in the XRD patterns of all these samples (Fig. S4).

Inspired by the advantageous structural features, the electrocatalysis HER performances of samples were evaluated in alkaline solution. The polarization curves of CoP-NCDs-0.5/NF with those of CoP/NF, bare Ni foam, and 20 wt% Pt/C on Ni foam (Pt–C/NF) are presented in Fig. 3 A. As expected, Pt/C catalyst exhibits the most favorable HER activity with a nearly zero overpotential. To achieve the current density of 10 mA cm−2, a small overpotential of 103 mV is required for the CoP-NCDs-0.5/NF, which is much lower than CoP/NF (151 mV) and bare Ni foam (290 mV). The corresponding Tafel slope of CoP-NCDs-0.5/NF is measured as 92 mV dec−1 (Fig. 3B), which is larger than that of Pt/C (70 mV dec−1), but superior to that of CoP/NF (125 mV dec−1) and bare Ni foam (166 mV dec−1), justifying its much faster HER kinetics. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) spectra in Fig. S5 show that the CoP-NCDs-0.5/NF exhibits a much smaller charge transfer resistance (Rct) than those of CoP/NF and bare Ni foam, further indicating the low charge transfer resistance and promoted HER kinetics. In addition, Fig. S6 shows that CoP-NCDs-0.5/NF have the largest Cdl value (71.1 mF cm−2, in proportion to the electrochemically active surface area, ECSA), which is nearly 2.4 times than that of pure CoP/NF (29.9 mF cm−2), implying more HER active sites on CoP-NCDs/NF than CoP/NF. These findings suggest that the introduction of NCDs can increase not only the electrical conductivity but also the number of active sites on the catalysts, thereby greatly improving the electrocatalytic performances. Fig. S7 displays the PL spectra of NCDs and CoP-NCDs-0.5. Compared with pure NCDs, the PL intensity of CoP-NCDs-0.5 remarkably declined, indicating that there is an electronic interaction between NCDs and CoP [31,45]. Moreover, CoP-NCDs-0.5/NF displays good stability with an almost overlapped LSV curve after 1000 cycles (Fig. 3C). The chronoamperometry measurement further shows that the current loss is negligible after 24 h HER test at an overpotential of 132 mV (Fig. 3C inset). SEM (Fig. S8A), TEM (Fig. S8B) and XRD (Fig. S8C) results confirm the excellent long-term stability of CoP-NCDs-0.5/NF. Additionally, CoP-NCDs/NF with different NCDs concentration (from 0 to 1.0 mg mL−1, Fig. S9) are evaluated by LSV. The results indicate that HER activity does not increase linearly with the NCDs amount. Conceivably, too much of NCDs will cover the active sites of CoP, while little NCDs dosage will hamper the roles of NCDs in facilitating the charge transfer/transport and reaction kinetics during the electrolysis process. Therefore, appropriate amount of NCDs is crucial to modulate the electronic property and geometry structure of the metal phosphide electrocatalysts simultaneously.

Interestingly, the fabricated catalyst shows high catalytic activity on OER as well, as shown in Fig. 3D. The CoP-NCDs-0.5/NF exhibits outstanding catalytic performance with an overpotential of 226 mV at 10 mA cm−2, which outperforms CoP/NF (326 mV) and bare Ni foam (438 mV), but slightly inferior to RuO2 (191 mV). Additionally, the Tafel slope of CoP-NCDs-0.5/NF (86 mV dec−1) is much lower than RuO2 (121 mV dec−1), CoP/NF (152 mV dec−1), and Ni foam (176 mV dec−1) (Fig. 3E). Meanwhile, the EIS results show the smallest charge transfer resistance of CoP-NCDs-0.5/NF, which directly contributed to its high catalytic activity (Fig. S10). The fitted Cdl values from CV curves also indicate that the CoP-NCDs-0.5/NF catalyst holds more active sites (Fig. S11). Furthermore, the excellent OER stability has also been confirmed by the cyclic test and chronoamperometry measurement (Fig. 3F). SEM (Fig. S12A), TEM (Fig. S12B) and XRD results (Fig. S12C) reveal that the fungus-like microspheres morphology and structure of the CoP-NCDs-0.5/NF can be well preserved due to the protective effect of NCDs, while the CoP/NF was partly dissolved and agglomerated after OER durability test (Fig. S13). These results demonstrate the outstanding OER properties of CoP-NCDs/NF in 1.0 M KOH. The OER activities of CoP with different NCDs concentration were also evaluated, as shown in Fig. S14. As expected, a higher or lower NCDs concentration also leads to the poor OER performance.

There are two main aspects contribute to such remarkable electrocatalytic performances of the CoP-NCDs/NF composites. Firstly, the morphological changes of each samples result in differences on the specific surface areas (SSAs), as shown in nitrogen adsorption-desorption measurements (Fig. S15). The specific fungus-like CoP-NCDs-0.5/NF with luxuriant branches has the largest SSA of 158.66 m2 g−1, which is higher than the CoP/NF with regular nanoneedles (104.42 m2 g−1), the CoP-NCDs-0.2/NF with limited branches (123.39 m2 g−1) and the spherical CoP-NCDs-1.0/NF with unduly crisscrossed branches (89.67 m2 g−1). The high SSA of the products provide larger active surface area as well as more contact sites with the electrolyte during electrocatalytic reactions.

Secondly, the strong chemically coupling between CoP and NCDs plays a critical role on facilitating the interfacial electron transfer between the reactants in electrolyte and the catalyst [46,47]. The chemical states of the as-prepared catalysts are characterized by the XPS technique. As shown in Fig. 4 A, the survey spectra verify the existence of Co, P, C, N, O in CoP-NCDs. Whereas the full-scale XPS spectrum of CoP displays the only presence of Co, P, C, and O elements, which demonstrate that the N element in CoP-NCDs composites is originated from NCDs. Fig. 4B–C shows the comparison of high-resolution XPS spectra of Co 2p and P 2p of the CoP-NCDs-0.5/NF and CoP/NF. In the high-resolution Co 2p XPS spectra of CoP-NCDs-0.5/NF (Fig. 4B), the peaks located at 778.0 and 793.5 eV arise from metallic Co 2p3/2 and Co 2p1/2, respectively. Meanwhile the two peaks at 780.8 and 797.1 eV are ascribed to 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 of Co2+, respectively [48]. Furthermore, in the high-resolution P 2p XPS spectra of CoP-NCDs-0.5/NF, the binding energies of 129.0 and 129.8 eV originate from P 2p3/2 and P 2p1/2 of phosphides (Pδ−), respectively, while the peak at 133.2 eV corresponds to the oxidized P species (P–O). On the other hand, the N 1s spectra of CoP-NCDs-0.5/NF can be deconvoluted into pyridinic N (398.7 eV), pyrrolic N (400.0 eV), and graphitic N (401.6 eV) (Fig. 4D). Take together with the results of Fig. 4 and S16, the Co 2p and P 2p peaks in all CoP-NCDs/NF samples are down-shifted compared with CoP/NF, while the location of N 1s peaks shifts toward higher binding energy compared with NCDs (Fig. S1E), indicating a strong electron transfer interaction from NCDs to CoP [29]. This implies an increase of electronic density in CoP after combination with NCDs. Therefore, the more negatively charged P atoms with a strong electrostatic affinity serving as protons acceptors promoted the generation of hydrogen in the HER process [49,50]. In addition, the XPS spectra of NCDs-CoP/NF after OER stability test showed that the surface of NCDs-CoP was slightly oxidized into metal oxide/hydroxide (Fig. S17), which were generally considered as the real active species of transition metal phosphides-based catalysts in OER process [51–53]. In Raman spectroscopy (Fig. S18), the G band of NCDs was found to be red shifted after combined with CoP, indicating a strong interaction between NCDs and CoP and an electron transfer from NCDs to CoP [29,54,55]. Additionally, the increased ID/IG intensity ratio (from 1.02 to 1.18) reveals the increased density of defects after composite formation. Thus, all these observations suggest that the NCDs were introduced into the electrocatalysts successfully and could effectively couple with the CoP ingredients in the fungus-like CoP-NCDs.

Considering the highly efficient catalytic ability and stability of CoP-NCDs/NF in HER and OER processes, a two-electrode alkaline cell with CoP-NCDs-0.5/NF as both anode and cathode electrode was assembled for overall water splitting test (Fig. 5 A). Surprisingly, for the CoP-NCDs-0.5/NF||CoP-NCDs-0.5/NF, the cell voltage to achieve the current density of 10 mA cm−2 is as low as 1.55 V, which is much smaller than CoP/NF||CoP/NF, bare NF||NF, and some other comparable cells reported in the literatures (Table S1). Moreover, the electrolyzer with a current density of 10 or 15 mA cm−2 can be durably driven by the CoP-NCDs-0.5/NF||CoP-NCDs-0.5/NF for more than 24 h at the voltage of 1.55 and 1.60 V, respectively (Fig. 5B), displaying its good long-term stability during the overall water splitting process.

In summary, we demonstrate a novel CDs-oriented method for the controlled growth of unique fungus-like CoP microspheres on Ni foam (CoP-NCDs/NF). With the high specific surface area and the strong interactions between NCDs and CoP, such electrocatalyst exhibits quite high catalytic activities and stabilities for both HER and OER. CoP-NCDs/NF can thus drive the current density of 10 mA cm−2 at only 103 mV and 226 mV, respectively. More importantly, with CoP-NCDs/NF as both anode and cathode, a two electrodes cell for overall water splitting needs only a very low cell voltage of 1.55 V to achieve the current density of 10 mA cm−2. Although there is still a long way to go to fully understand the CDs-oriented growth mechanism, this work lays a foundation for the preparation of CDs-based electrocatalysts for efficient water-splitting and other energy conversion systems.

Hui Liu: Writing – original draftWriting - original draft, Methodology, Data curation. Zihui Liu: Preparation. Yang Wang: Writing – review & editingWriting - review & editing. Jinqiang Zhang: Writing – review & editingWriting - review & editing. Zhongxue Yang: Characterization. Qingshan Zhao: Investigation. Hui Ning: Discussion. Linjie Zhi: Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 52072409, U1662113, U20A20131), Shandong Provincial Natural Science Foundation (ZR2018ZC1458), and Taishan Scholar Project (No. ts201712020).
